details.text: Derivative: غَرْقًا here is a Mafhoom Mutlaq Bi-Ghairi Lafzhihi (مَفْهُوم مُطْلَق بِغَيْرِ لَفْظِهِ). It means a word which is used to add emphasis to the word that precedes it with a word that is not from the same root as the original word, in the case where the usual usage of the Mafhoom Mutlaq is to use a word from the same root as the word it is giving emphasis to i.e., instead of the word نَزْعًا (Naz’an), the word غَرْقًا (Gharqan) is used. غَرْقًا (Gharqan) which means to plunge, be drowned or become immersed implies the meaning of going in deep and here it is to emphasise the force of the yanking that takes place.

Derivative: النَّازِعَاتِ comes from نَزَعَ which means to yank or pull something out or off with force and violence. Here Allah swears by النَّازِعَاتِ “Those who pull out”. The main view is that النَّازِعَاتِ refers to the angels. A secondary view is that they refer to the نُجُوم (Nujoom), stars which are being yanked out of the sky. A third opinion is that it is referring to the winds which yank things out as the verse follows the same pattern used often to describe the winds, because the feminine form is being used whilst the Angels (Malaa’ikah) are not feminine and the masculine is used often with them in the Qur’an, and because the verb نَزَعَ has been used in conjunction with the winds in the Qur’an, amongst other reasons.

Derivative: الْحَافِرَةِ is used with the verb to return to mean: To return back to where one started, came from or to his original state. Here it refers to being brought back to life: “Will we indeed be returned back to the starting point or original condition i.e., be resurrected and brought back to life?”.
Derivative: نَّخِرَةً refers to bones that are emptied in the inside due to decay leaving nothing but a shell i.e., decayed and rotting.

Derivative: قَالُوا – In classical Arabic the past tense (قَالُوا – They said) was used to indicate to something that occurred once or a few occasions whilst the present tense (يَقُولونَ – They say) would be used for something that occurred frequently and implies continuity.
Derivative: كَرَّة refers to a "return" and also an “attack”.
Derivative: زَجْرَةٌ a single time of زَجْر which refers to a form of very loud and severe yelling accompanied by anger. It has been said to refer also to the final blowing of the trumpet.

Derivative: السَّاهِرَةِ – سَهِرَ literally means sleeplessness. سَاهِرَة was used to refer to an open field because when someone is in such an open place he is potentially exposed to being seen or to danger and as such he is unable to sleep in it. It has also been said that it refers to the surface of the ground, the place that everyone will be gathered for resurrection and also to a state of permanent sleeplessness as from that time there will be no more sleep.

Derivative: حَدِيثُ means news or speech or information about something. It is used when the subject matter is manifest and it is also used when something is so old that it has become forgotten and becomes like something new when spoken about again.

Derivative: الْمُقَدَّسِ is from تَقْدِيس (Taqdees) which means to speak of Allah in a way that is appropriate and fitting for Allah and to remove from Allah any attributions that are not appropriate for Him. الْمُقَدَّسِ can refer to a place that is specified for declaring the perfection of Allah, a place that is sanctified or made sacred.
Derivative: نَادَا – نَادَى – نداءًا (Nidaa’) means to call out to someone loudly.
Derivative: طَغَىٰ – طُغْيان (Tughyaan): It means to rebel. This is used to describe when someone or something crosses and rebels against the limits that have been set.

Derivative: تَزَكَّىٰ is originally تَتَزَكَّى but the shortened version is used here and it use can imply a lessening of its impact within the sentence it is used in. It means to cleanse and purify oneself. تَزْكِيَة (Tazkiyah) means to cleanse the evils, flaws and rebelliousness that exist within oneself.

فَقُلْ هَلْ لَكَ إِلَىٰ أَنْ تَزَكَّىٰ

- The use of تَزَكَّىٰ the shortened version of تَتَزَكَّى can add an implication that would make the Ayah mean: “Have you not got any inclination within yourself to be purified even a little bit” i.e., not even complete purification but any purification and cleansing of oneself.

- This expression relates to one’s desire to bring something out in terms of goodness. In this context it means: “Have you not got any desire or inclination within you for you to be cleansed and purified i.e., to better yourself”.

Derivative: أَهْدِيَ here means “That I may guide you”. When attached to إِلَى (Ilaa – To) the word هَدَى – يَهِدِي - هِدَايَة means “To guide along with you” i.e., it implies to the offering of support. “So that I will guide you to (أَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ) your Lord” implies to teach you and support you to reach it i.e., the guidance to his Lord.
Derivative: تَخْشَىٰ from خَشِيَ means to fear something that is huge and of great consequence.

وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشَىٰ

- This verse is connected to the statement in the previous verse هَلْ لَكَ إِلَىٰ أَنْ “Have you not got any inclination within yourself” which is evident from the Fat’hah (َ) in أَهْدِيَكَ which means that it is مَنْصُوب (Mansoob – Accusative case) due to the أَنْ in the previous verse.

Derivative: النَّاشِطَاتِ based on the understanding that the Angels are the subject of the speech and that the verb نَشِطَ refers to undoing a knot with ease, refers to: “Those Angels that seamlessly, smoothly, with ease and without any effort or force take out the soul from the human”. If it is in reference to winds, it would mean: “Those winds that bring ease and relief”.
Derivative: نَشْطًا (Nasht) can refer in the Arabic language to a knot that is easy to undo and is undone without making any effort.
Derivative: عَصَىٰ means to disobey, defy to rebel and is used for wilful disobedience.
Derivative: أَدْبَرَ means to turn one’s back.
Derivative: يَسْعَىٰ from سَعَى – سَعْي refers to moving at a pace that is faster than walking but slower than running.

ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ يَسْعَىٰ

- Combining the two meanings of أَدْبَرَ (to turn one’s back) and يَسْعَىٰ (to move at a fast pace) provides the image of someone quickly pacing up and down (i.e., turning) in anxiousness thinking about how to deal with their problems.
- Most of the Mufassireen however have understood أَدْبَرَ to refer to turning one’s back in opposition and rejection and understood يَسْعَىٰ to mean to strive and make effort i.e., to counter Musa (as).
Derivative: حَشَرَ means to gather but implies a forcefulness or from a position of power and authority as the original usage of حَشَرَ relates to the herding of animals and gathering them together.

Derivative: نَكَالَ was used for chains that were placed on the ankles of prisoners to restrict and control their movement. It was used as a message for those who saw them to deter them from challenging the authority or acting against the law. It was a means for planting fear in a population. From this meaning نَكَالَ (Nakaal) is used to refer to an exemplary punishment and a severe punishment that makes an example out of someone.
Derivative: أَخَذَ means to take or seize. Here it is used in reference to being seized with a punishment i.e., for it to be inflicted.
- This can refer to the exemplary punishment that he will be seized with in this life and the hereafter, or the exemplary punishment for the first and last generations to take heed of.

Derivative: عِبْرَةً is commonly translated as a “lesson” and a “warning” to take heed from. In origin it comes from the verb عَبَرَ which means to cross over, like crossing a river i.e., movement from one place that is crossed over to another. عَبِرَ means to shed tears in the case where the tear passes across the eyelid to fall down the face. عِبْرَةً can therefore be understood to mean something that contains within it enough reason to shed tears.
Derivative: بَنَا – بَنَى – بِنَاء means to build and construct something where one brick, part or segment meshes and conjoins onto another to form a seamless construction.
Derivative: السَّمَاءُ comes from the root of سمو which refers to everything that is above you.
Derivative: سَمْكَ means its highest or top part.
Derivative: سَوَّا – سَوَّى – تَسْوِيَة means to level, even and smooth out, to balance and also to mould, shape and fashion to perfection.
Derivative: أَغْطَشَ means when darkness comes, it becomes dim and difficult to see clearly.

Derivative: السَّابِحَاتِ from سَبَحَ which means to swim in a rapid smooth manner which is in reference to the movements of the Angels. If it is in relation to the clouds then it refers to the movement of the clouds.
Derivative: دَحَا – يَدْحُو – دَحْو means to spread out, make level and smooth.
Derivative: مَرْعَا – مَرْعَى is ظَرْف المَكان (Adverb of a place). It comes from رَعْي which means to put animals to graze in a pasture. مَرْعَى refers to land for grazing and farming.
Derivative: أَرْسَا means to anchor and fix firmly.
Derivative: مَتَاعًا is from مَتَعَ – يَمْتَعُ – مَتْعًا which means to use and enjoy. مَتَاع means something that one can make use of and benefit from.

Derivative: جَاءَتِ means to come or to arrive. It is from جَاءَ. جَاءَ and أَتَى both mean to come but the first is used to indicate a coming or arrival that has a stronger import. جَاءَ implies a grand or significant arrival, in contrast to أَتَى which implies an easy arrival.

Derivative: الطَّامَّةُ – طَمٌّ in Arabic is used to refer to something becoming completely full or being completely engulfed. الطَّامَّةُ refers to the calamity that is so engulfing and overwhelming that there is no escape from it.
Derivative: يَتَذَكَّرُ here means that he will thoroughly remember everything. The complete recollection of everything is capture in يَتَذَكَّرُ.
Derivative: سَعَىٰ means to rush towards and the efforts and endeavours that one makes i.e., what he pursued in life.
Derivative: بُرِّزَتِ – Means to be brought out and shown i.e., to be made manifest before one’s eyes.

Derivative: الْجَحِيمُ – جَهُمَ is used for a lion that is staring at you with hungry eyes. It is also used for a blaze that is so intense that it almost seems to be staring at you. الْجَحِيمُ is one of the words of hellfire.
Derivative: آثَرَ means to give preference to something or someone.
Derivative: الْمَأْوَىٰ comes from آَوَى – إِيواء which means to seek a place of refuge in. الْمَأْوَىٰ is a place of refuge.

Derivative: السَّابِقَاتِ comes from سَبَقَ which means to race and to take the lead. In reference to the Angels, it could mean: “That the Angels compete to undertake the commands of their creator and to deliver the souls from the humans”. In relation to winds, it would mean: “The clouds that compete i.e., move faster than others”.

Derivative: الْهَوَىٰ refers to the base, impulsive and empty desire which seems powerful at the time of its emergence but when it subsides there is nothing there i.e., it was not established upon a substantial basis.

Derivative: النَّفْسَ (the self): The use of النَّفْسَ (An-Nafs) with Alif and Meem (ال) which means the Nafs, instead of stating his Nafs, implies that the Nafs is like a separate entity to the person that needs to be battled against.
Derivative: مَقَامَ is a ظَرْف (Zharf - Adverb) of time and place. It can therefore mean the “place of standing” and also “the time of standing”.
Derivative: مُنْتَهَا – مُنْتَهَى comes from اِنْتَهَى – اِنْتِهاء which refers to the end, end point or conclusion of something.
Derivative: مُرْسَا means its time of anchoring i.e., the time that has been firmly set (for it (i.e., the final hour) to occur).
Derivative: عَشِيَّةً is the time when the sun begins to set up until Maghrib (sunset) i.e., an afternoon.
Derivative: ضُحَا – ضُحَى is the time after sunrise until noon i.e., a morning.

Derivative: الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ – مُدَبِّر (Mudabbir) is an اِسمُ الفَاعِل (Ism-ul-Fa’il – Noun of the doer) from دَبَّرَ – تَدْبِيرًا. تَدْبِير includes the meanings of planning, managing and executing or taking care of a matter. الْمُدَبِّرَاتِ therefore refers to those (Angels) who execute the command or affair of Allah (أَمْرًا) in a diligent and thorough manner. If it relates to winds, then it would refer to “Those winds which organise the distribution of the sources of life on earth according to Allah’s plan and decree (أَمْرًا)” i.e., rain clouds, pollen and seed etc.

Derivative: الرَّاجِفَةُ refers to that thing which is meant to shake or convulse. الرَّاجِفَةُ is therefore being used in place of the earth as if the earth itself is being called by the name of what is bound and decreed to inevitably happen to it. This has been explained to refer to the first blowing of the trumpet.
Derivative: تَرْجُفُ is from رَجَفَ which means to shake or convulse. It refers to an unstable and uncomfortable motion.

Derivative: الرَّادِفَةُ is from رَدَفَ which means that which follows or comes next i.e., is subsequent to something. رَادِف is used to refer to the person who rides behind another on an animal. الرَّادِفَةُ therefore refers to that which follows what preceded it i.e., the shaking mentioned in the previous verb which will be followed by another shaking that is subsequent to it. This has been explained to be in reference to the second blowing of the trumpet.

Derivative: وَاجِفَةٌ refers to a fear mixed with discomfort where the heart is pounding due to that fear. It is also used when you spur a horse on, and its heart starts pounding or racing. وَاجِفَةٌ can therefore carry the meaning that their hearts (قُلُوبٌ) will “be beating out of their chests (وَاجِفَةٌ)”.
Derivative: خَاشِعَةٌ means to be overwhelmed and humbled due to the fear of what is being presented before them.
